key_insight: Glass can be shaped and frozen to mimic tissue-like deformation, offering a controllable physical analog for studying how epithelial tissues stretch and fail.
engineering_relevance: This provides an accessible experimental platform for probing deformation, ductile-to-brittle transitions, and geometry changes relevant to soft tissue mechanics and fracture behavior.
method: Shape glass into tissue-like monolayers in a glass studio
Heat to the material’s process temperature and apply controlled lateral or radial stretching
Rapidly cool to arrest the deformed state for measurement and comparison with tensegrity-based simulations
evidence_signal: Experiments quantified changes in cell area and eccentricity under stretch, and the tensegrity model reproduced the main deformation trends but underpredicted the eccentricity increase.
watch_item: Because the model underestimates the observed eccentricity changes, it should be treated as a qualitative-to-semiquantitative analog rather than a fully predictive tissue surrogate.
paper_title: Glass-based physical models for tissue mechanics
